لطفا متن زیر را به زبان انگلیسی و آکادمیک بازنویسی کن به طوری که معنی و مفهوم آن حفظ شود و تغییر نکند. All electrochemical measurements including cyclic voltammetry (CV),
chronoamperometry, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), were performed
with an Autolab 204 electrochemical workstation in a three-electrode configuration.

All electrochemical measurements, including cyclic voltammetry (CV), chronoamperometry, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), were conducted using an Autolab 204 electrochemical workstation with a three-electrode setup.

FT-IR spectra were attained by using a Perkin Elmer FT-IR spectrometer (spectrum
100) from wavenumber of 400 cm-1 to 4000 cm-1.
FT-IR spectra ranging from 400 cm-1 to 4000 cm-1 were obtained using a Perkin Elmer FT-IR spectrometer (spectrum 100).

In order to study the electrochemical mechanism of the redox reaction, cyclic voltammograms (CVs) are usually recorded on the surface of the modified electrode at different potential scan rates. The nature of the electrode process on the surface of 3DG-CNTN was investigated for MTX (0.1 mM) at pH 6.0 in various scan rates (in the range of 50 to 350 mVs-1 ) and is shown in Fig. 8A. As can be seen, the oxidation peak current shifts positively with increasing the scan rate. The plot of peak current (Ip,a) versus the scan rate (υ) shows a linear relationship with the regression equation described as follows (Fig. 8B):

To investigate the electrochemical mechanism of the redox reaction, cyclic voltammograms (CVs) were recorded on the surface of the modified electrode at different potential scan rates. The behavior of the electrode process on the surface of 3DG-CNTN was examined for MTX (0.1 mM) at pH 6.0 using various scan rates (ranging from 50 to 350 mVs-1), as depicted in Figure 8A. It is observed that the oxidation peak current shifts towards higher values with an increase in the scan rate. The relationship between the peak current (Ip,a) and scan rate (υ) is linear, as shown in Figure 8B, and can be described by the following regression equation:
These observations indicate an adsorption-controlled process on the surface of the modified.
Based on these observations, it can be inferred that the process occurring on the surface of the modified electrode is controlled by adsorption.
CVs of 0.1 mM MTX on the surface of 3DG-CNTN modified electrode at different scan rates in 0.1M PBS (pH 6.0); (B) the plot of anodic peak current (Ip,a) versus scan rate (υ).

Cyclic voltammograms (CVs) were recorded for 0.1 mM MTX on the surface of a 3DG-CNTN modified electrode at various scan rates in 0.1M PBS (pH 6.0). The plot of anodic peak current (Ip,a) versus scan rate (υ) is shown in Figure B.

The calibration curve for Aza determination was established by recording the cathodic peak currents of Aza at different concentrations using AgNPs-G/PG modified electrode (Fig. 9A). Under the previously mentioned optimum conditions (0.1 M phosphate buffer solution of pH 6.0 and an accumulation time of 50 s), the reduction peak currents were proportional to Aza concentrations over 3 intervals in the range of 7 × 10− 7 to 1 × 10− 4 mol L− 1

In order to establish the calibration curve for Aza determination, the cathodic peak currents of Aza were measured at different concentrations using an AgNPs-G/PG modified electrode (Figure 9A). The reduction peak currents were found to be proportional to the concentrations of Aza over three intervals in the range of 7 × 10−7 to 1 × 10−4 mol L−1, under the optimal conditions mentioned earlier (0.1 M phosphate buffer solution of pH 6.0 and an accumulation time of 50 s).
The linear regression equation for the first region was:
The linear regression equation for the first region of the calibration curve was:

Based on the linear plot of peak current versus Aza's concentration (Fig. 9B), a detection limit (S/N = 3) of 68 nM is obtained for Aza determination, indicating the system is quite sensitive toward Aza.

Based on the linear plot of the peak current versus the concentration of Aza (Figure 9B), a detection limit of 68 nM (S/N = 3) is achieved for Aza determination. This indicates that the system is highly sensitive to Aza.

CVs for various concentrations of Aza in the range of (up to down) 0.7–100 μmol L− 1 in 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (pH 6.0) and (B) corresponding linear calibration curve of peak current vs. Aza concentration; scan rate was 100 mV s− 1.

Cyclic voltammograms (CVs) were obtained for different concentrations of Aza in the range of 0.7 to 100 μmol L−1 in a 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (pH 6.0). The CVs were recorded at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1. 

Figure A: CVs for various concentrations of Aza in the stated range.

Figure B: The corresponding linear calibration curve of peak current plotted against Aza concentration.
Investigation of the electrochemical behavior of ISPN
The electrochemical behavior of ISPN was studied and analyzed.
Effect of the potential scan rate
The effect of the potential scan rate on the electrochemical behavior of ISPN was investigated.
